podman machine start
podman compose --file compose.yaml down --remove-orphans
podman compose --file compose.yaml up --build
podman compose --file compose.yaml up storage-db storage-service keycloak eureka-server tempo
podman compose logs -f storage-service
podman compose logs -f keycloak
podman ps --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}"



5) End‑to‑end verification with Postman / cURL
5.1 Get Access Tokens from Keycloak

Use the Password grant (direct access grants) against the storage-service client (since you set "directAccessGrantsEnabled": true there). From your host:
If you prefer Authorization Code flow, keep standardFlowEnabled: true and use Postman’s OAuth 2.0 flow with the same client_id/secret and redirect URI.

Admin token

curl -s -X POST \
  http://localhost:8089/realms/microservices-fundamentals-realm/protocol/openid-connect/token \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=password" \
  -d "client_id=storage-service" \
  -d "client_secret=storage-service-secret" \
  -d "username=admin_user" \
  -d "password=admin" | jq -r .access_token

curl.exe -s -X POST `
  http://localhost:8089/realms/microservices-fundamentals-realm/protocol/openid-connect/token `
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" `
  -d "grant_type=password&client_id=storage-service&client_secret=storage-service-secret&username=admin_user&password=admin"

→ 200 OK

This matrix verifies your role rules and proper 401/403 handling.

6) How the OAuth 2.0 flow works in this setup (mapping to your diagram)

Client → Authorization Server: Your Postman/cURL asks Keycloak for an access token (password grant in our example).
Authorization Server → Client: Keycloak returns a JWT access token containing claims (iss, sub, realm_access.roles, scope, etc.).
Client → Resource Server: Client sends API request with Authorization: Bearer <token> to Storage Service.
Resource Server → (local validation): Storage Service validates the JWT using the issuer’s JWK set (no round-trip needed per request).
If valid & authorized: Spring Security resolves roles to ROLE_admin/ROLE_user, evaluates your requestMatchers and proceeds to controller; else returns 401/403.


You do not need a custom filter for JWT validation—Spring Security’s resource server adds the necessary filter. Your custom Converter is the right place to map claims → authorities.


7) Common pitfalls & troubleshooting


401 Unauthorized

Missing/expired token.
The token’s iss does not match your configured issuer-uri. Ensure your service’s issuer-uri equals the token’s iss. In Docker, use http://keycloak:8089/.... Outside Docker, either use localhost or set jwk-set-uri.
JWK discovery blocked by networking (container cannot resolve keycloak). Use service name/network alias or set jwk-set-uri explicitly.



403 Forbidden

Token is valid, but role missing:

normal_user has only user role → POST/DELETE should be 403 (expected).
Your converter doesn’t see roles (e.g., you removed realm roles and only mapped top-level roles): update converter as above.


You’re using client roles only → ensure converter reads resource_access.<clientId>.roles.



No roles inside token

In Keycloak, ensure:

User has the realm role.
Client has access to realm roles (by default yes).
If using client roles, user must be granted them (via group/role mapping).





Service runs locally but Keycloak in Docker

Use spring.security.oauth2.resourceserver.jwt.jwk-set-uri=http://localhost:8089/realms/microservices-fundamentals-realm/protocol/openid-connect/certs
Or add keycloak to your /etc/hosts → 127.0.0.1 keycloak (only if Keycloak is bound to localhost as that hostname).
